roles.Contrarian: Challenge assumptions and default positions. Default to opposing the most commonly assumed answer.
roles.Optimist: Favor constructive, positive pathways when justified. Lean toward Yes when a concrete pathway exists.
roles.Utilitarian: Maximize aggregate outcomes. Answer based purely on who is helped vs harmed and by how much.
roles.Absolutist: Follow strict rules and established precedent. Default to No when rules are unclear or incomplete.

results.question: Should a university apply the same attendance policy to every student, even if some have serious but hard-to-document personal problems?

- Cumulative negative effects on that student's outcomes

**Benefits from flexible rubric:**
- Student receives accurate feedback reflecting their actual progress

When someone has a strong excuse, should the fact that others may not get the same excuse still stop an exception, yes, no, or maybe?
Should institutions prefer clear rules over flexible judgment, even if flexibility sometimes leads to better individual outcomes, yes, no, or maybe?
If a rule is meant to be fair in general, should that mean it should not bend for rare hard cases, yes, no, or maybe?
Should consistency be valued more than mercy when people disagree about what counts as a special circumstance, yes, no, or maybe?
When a rule is applied unevenly, should the main solution be to make everyone follow it more strictly, yes, no, or maybe?
If following a rule feels unjust in one case, should the rule still be respected to avoid weakening it overall, yes, no, or maybe?
Should people accept some unfairness in individual cases if that is the cost of having the same standard for everyone, yes, no, or maybe?
When a rule is challenged by a very compelling personal situation, should the default still be to say no to exceptions, yes, no, or maybe?
Should a consistent rule matter more than whether a person’s situation seems unusually unlucky, yes, no, or maybe?
If a rule has to be explained differently each time to make sense, should it still be kept the same for everyone, yes, no, or maybe?
Should a person’s good intentions matter less than whether the rule was broken the same way others would have broken it, yes, no, or maybe?
When one exception might encourage more exceptions, should that be enough reason to refuse it, yes, no, or maybe?
Should the same standard apply even if some people argue that life is too complicated for simple rules, yes, no, or maybe?
If a rule treats most people fairly but not every person equally well, should consistency still take priority, yes, no, or maybe?
When someone points to an unusual hardship, should that usually change the rule’s application, yes, no, or maybe?
Should rules be designed so that exceptions are rare enough to almost never matter, yes, no, or maybe?
If a decision feels more humane when made case by case, should that be avoided in favor of the same rule each time, yes, no, or maybe?
Should equal treatment matter more than making room for personal circumstances that are hard to compare, yes, no, or maybe?
When the same rule leads to different levels of burden for different people, should the rule still stay the same, yes, no, or maybe?
If everyone knows the rule in advance, should surprise exceptions still be discouraged, yes, no, or maybe?
Should people prefer a rule that is slightly less fair in special cases if it is much more consistent overall, yes, no, or maybe?
When a strict rule causes regret, should that alone be a reason not to make exceptions next time, yes, no, or maybe?
Should a decision be based mainly on the rule itself rather than on how deserving one particular person seems, yes, no, or maybe?
If two wrongs look different in context, should they still be treated the same under the rule, yes, no, or maybe?
Should consistency matter more than empathy when the choice is between one clear rule and one compelling exception, yes, no, or maybe?
When the facts of a case feel unusual, should the safest approach still be to apply the rule exactly as written, yes, no, or maybe?
Should a rule be trusted more than individual judgment even when the rule seems a little too blunt for real life, yes, no, or maybe?
If a person asks for mercy, should the answer usually depend on the rule rather than the details of their story, yes, no, or maybe?
Should the same behavior always lead to the same outcome, even if the surrounding circumstances are very different, yes, no, or maybe?
When a rule protects against unfair favoritism, should that be enough to keep it from being bent, yes, no, or maybe?
If an exception would feel right in one case but wrong in another similar case, should the rule stay unchanged, yes, no, or maybe?
Should decision-makers avoid personal sympathy when deciding whether to apply a rule, yes, no, or maybe?
When a rule is widely known, should people be expected to follow it even if their own situation seems exceptional, yes, no, or maybe?
Should a clear rule generally outweigh the appeal of handling each situation in a more personal way, yes, no, or maybe?
